title: The Agentic Loop
outcome: Drive an agent loop off the response's stop_reason, name the completion signals that only look reliable, and tell an agent apart from a workflow.
objectives: Sequence one iteration of the loop, from request to the next request
Map each stop_reason value to the action the caller must take
Separate deterministic completion evidence from inferences about it
Decide whether a workload wants a model-driven loop or a fixed sequence

steps.title: The loop is a state machine, not a function call
steps.body:
A request goes out carrying the message history and the tool definitions. The response comes back carrying a stop_reason. That field — not the prose the model wrote alongside it — decides what happens next: execute the requested tool and re-enter the loop, or surface the result and stop.

The difference from a hard-coded decision tree is who chooses the next step. In a fixed flow the caller picks which tool runs. In an agentic loop the model picks, using the tool results accumulated so far. The caller's remaining job is bookkeeping: run the tool that was requested, append the result to the history, and resend the whole history.

That bookkeeping is easy to get subtly wrong, and every way of getting it wrong looks like a model problem from the outside.

callout.body: Caller's job in a fixed workflow: decide what happens next. Caller's job in an agentic loop: execute, append, resend. If your loop code contains business logic about which tool should run, you have written a workflow with extra steps.

steps.tip: The API is stateless. Nothing you fail to write back into the message history exists on the next turn.

steps.title: Sequence one iteration
steps.prompt: One iteration of an agentic loop, from a fresh request to the point where the next request goes out. Put the stages in order.

items.label: Send the message history plus tool definitions to the API
Receive the response object
Read stop_reason off the response
Execute the tool the model requested
Append the tool result to the message history
Send the extended history back for the next iteration

why.send: The tool definitions have to be in the request or the model has nothing it can call.
why.receive: Nothing is decidable before the response object exists.
why.inspect: This is the branch point. Every anti-pattern in this module is a substitute for reading this field.
why.exec: The model emits a request to call a tool. It never makes the call itself — the caller does.
why.append: The API is stateless. A result that is not written into the history does not exist next turn.
why.resend: Full history every time, not a delta.

steps.hint: Two of these stages are pure bookkeeping and the loop is silently broken without them. Which stage produces the thing the next stage consumes?
steps.explanation: Every adjacency here is a real data dependency: you cannot append a result you have not produced, and you cannot resend a history you have not extended. There is no acceptable alternate ordering for this step.
steps.tip: The single most common broken implementation skips the append stage. The model then re-requests the same tool forever, and the author blames the model.

steps.title: stop_reason value to caller action
steps.prompt: A response has come back. For each stop_reason value, what does the caller do with it?

buckets.label: Continue the loop
buckets.blurb: Execute the requested tool, append the result, iterate

buckets.label: Turn is finished
buckets.blurb: Surface the result

buckets.label: Output was cut off
buckets.blurb: The response is incomplete

buckets.label: Caller's own protocol decides
buckets.blurb: The meaning is application-specific

items.text: stop_reason: "tool_use"
items.why: The model has emitted a tool-call request and is waiting on the result.

items.text: stop_reason: "end_turn"
items.why: The model reached a natural end. This is the only completion signal that is not an inference.

items.text: stop_reason: "max_tokens"
items.why: The output hit the ceiling. Treating this as completion ships a half-written answer as a finished one.

items.text: stop_reason: "stop_sequence"
items.why: A sequence the caller configured was emitted. Only the caller knows what it was supposed to mean.

steps.hint: Two of these mean the work is not finished, and they mean it for opposite reasons. One of them is a trap precisely because the text alongside it can read as a complete answer.
steps.explanation: The exam cares most about telling tool_use apart from end_turn — that is the loop's branch. The other two matter because mistaking either of them for completion is the failure mode: max_tokens ships a truncated answer, and stop_sequence hands control back without saying why.
steps.tip: Treat this as four values you must handle, not as the complete list of values that exist. Source the full set from the Messages API reference rather than from any study guide.

steps.title: Which completion signals are actually reliable?
steps.prompt: Your loop needs to know the agent has finished. Sort each candidate signal by whether it is deterministic evidence or an inference about it.

buckets.label: Reliable
buckets.blurb: Deterministic evidence

buckets.label: Unreliable
buckets.blurb: An inference, a guess, or a guard misread as a result

items.text: stop_reason equals end_turn
items.why: The API's own signal. Everything else on this list is a guess about it.

items.text: The assistant's text says the task is finished
items.why: Free text is generated, not asserted. A model can narrate completion in the middle of a workflow.

items.text: The iteration counter hit its cap
items.why: A cap is a runaway guard. Firing it means the loop failed to converge, not that it succeeded.

items.text: The response contains a text block instead of a tool-call block
items.why: It correlates with completion but infers the state from content shape, and it also reads as done on a truncated response.

items.text: The response's tool-call list is empty
items.why: Same defect. It infers the state instead of reading it, and cannot tell finished apart from cut off.

items.text: The model emitted the caller's configured stop sequence
items.why: The model has to remember to emit it. Probabilistic by construction.

items.text: A downstream validator confirmed the artifact the agent was asked to produce
items.why: Not a loop-control signal, but genuine evidence: it checks the world rather than the transcript. Pair it with end_turn, never substitute it.

steps.hint: Reliable does not mean 'it is an API field'. It means the signal cannot be wrong when the underlying state is different. One non-API item on this list clears that bar.
steps.explanation: The dividing line is deterministic evidence versus inference. Everything in the unreliable bucket reads the transcript and guesses at the state — and most of them cannot distinguish 'finished' from 'cut off'. The validator row is the hard one on purpose: it is reliable because it inspects the artifact itself, not because it comes from the API.
steps.tip: Parsing the model's prose for completion is a documented elimination rule: an answer that detects completion by reading text is almost always the wrong one.

steps.title: Agent or workflow?
steps.prompt: For each workload, decide whether the path is known in advance (a fixed sequence of prompts) or discovered during execution (a model-driven loop).

buckets.label: Workflow
buckets.blurb: Fixed sequence, path known in advance

buckets.label: Agent
buckets.blurb: Model-driven loop, path discovered during execution

items.text: Every incoming invoice runs the same extract, validate, enrich sequence
items.why: The steps never vary. A loop adds nondeterminism and buys nothing.

items.text: Find out why a nightly job started failing last week
items.why: Each finding changes what to look at next.

items.text: Translate each of 400 product blurbs, one call apiece
items.why: This is not even a chain, it is a map. Wrapping it in an agent is over-architecture.

items.text: Add test coverage to an unfamiliar module
items.why: The structure has to be mapped before the subtasks exist.

items.text: Generate a release note from a fixed changelog template
items.why: Deterministic input, deterministic template.

items.text: Reproduce a customer bug report with unclear steps
items.why: The reproduction path is the unknown.

items.text: Review a 3-file pull request against a written checklist
items.why: The checklist fixes the passes in advance.

items.text: Triage an alert that may be infrastructure, application, or data
items.why: The routing decision depends on evidence gathered along the way.

steps.hint: Ask whether you could draw the flowchart before running it.
steps.explanation: If the flowchart is drawable up front, it is a workflow, and building it as an agent trades determinism for nothing. If the next step genuinely depends on what the previous step found, a fixed chain cannot express the work. Volume is not the deciding factor — 400 identical translations are still a workflow.
steps.tip: Adding agency is the most common over-engineering answer on this exam. Constrain rather than add: the correct answer usually removes freedom the system did not need.

steps.title: Drill: reading the loop's branch point

title: Coordinators, Subagents & Context Handoff
outcome: State exactly what a subagent arrives with, write a delegation that carries its own context, and tell parallel work apart from work that only looks parallel.
objectives: Describe the coordinator's role in a hub-and-spoke system
Sort what a subagent has, what must be passed to it, and what it can fetch
Avoid both over-claiming and under-claiming what a subagent inherits
Decide which delegations can be spawned in one turn and which must be sequenced

steps.title: The coordinator owns the wires
steps.body:
A multi-agent system is a hub and spokes. The coordinator decomposes the work, decides which subagents are needed, delegates, aggregates what comes back, validates it, retries what failed, and reports.

Subagents do not talk to each other. Everything transits the hub. That constraint is not bureaucracy — it is what makes the system observable and gives failures exactly one place to be handled. A system where spoke A can call spoke B has no single point that knows what the run is doing.

The practical consequence is that a subagent's inbound channel is the delegation prompt. Whatever it needs is either written into that prompt, attached to its own configuration, or reachable through its own tools.

callout.body: A subagent starts fresh, but not empty. It does not get the coordinator's conversation history, the coordinator's earlier tool results, or a sibling's output. It does get its own configuration — its system prompt, the tools its definition grants it, and (in Claude Code) the project memory hierarchy. 'A subagent has nothing at all' is as wrong as 'a subagent sees what I see'.

steps.tip: Both directions of this claim are examinable. Over-claiming inheritance is the classic trap; under-claiming it is the trap that catches people who over-corrected.

steps.title: What does the subagent actually have?
steps.prompt: A coordinator is about to delegate. For each item, decide whether the subagent already has it, whether the coordinator must put it in the prompt, or whether the subagent can go and get it.

buckets.label: Has it
buckets.blurb: Present without any further action

buckets.label: Must be passed
buckets.blurb: Absent unless the coordinator writes it into the prompt

buckets.label: Can fetch it
buckets.blurb: Not present, but reachable with its own tools

items.text: The text of the delegation prompt
items.why: This is the entire inbound channel.

items.text: Its own system prompt and agent configuration
items.why: Attached to the subagent definition, not to the call.

items.text: The tool definitions its configuration grants it
items.why: Tools come from the definition. This is why 'a subagent inherits nothing' over-corrects.

items.text: Project conventions from the memory hierarchy the whole session loads
items.why: Standing project instructions are loaded for the subagent too. What it loses is the conversation, not the configuration.

items.text: The coordinator's conversation history
items.why: Isolated context. This is the single most-tested trap in the domain.

items.text: Tool results the coordinator collected earlier
items.why: They live in the coordinator's window only.

items.text: A sibling subagent's findings
items.why: Siblings never communicate directly. The hub relays.

items.text: The user's original request
items.why: It feels inherited. It is not.

items.text: Whatever it learned on a previous invocation
items.why: No memory across calls. Persist it yourself if you need it.

items.text: A file on disk, given its path in the prompt
items.why: The path must be passed; the bytes need not be. This is the large-artifact handoff.

items.text: A public URL named in the prompt
items.why: Given a fetch tool. Note the tool has to be in its scope, or this becomes must-pass.

steps.hint: Separate the two things a subagent arrives with: its configuration, and its conversation. One of those is inherited in full and one of those is empty.
steps.explanation: Configuration travels; conversation does not. Anything that lived in the coordinator's window — history, earlier results, a sibling's output, the user's own words — is gone unless written into the prompt. Anything attached to the subagent's definition is present. The can-fetch bucket is the cost-saver: pass a pointer, not a payload, and let the subagent pull only what it needs.
steps.tip: When you are unsure which bucket something is in, it is must-pass. Being wrong in that direction costs a few tokens; being wrong the other way costs a silent failure.

steps.title: One turn or several?
steps.prompt: The coordinator has eight pieces of work. Sort them by whether they can be spawned together in a single coordinator response or must wait for an earlier result.

buckets.label: Same turn
buckets.blurb: Spawn together in one coordinator response

buckets.label: Must sequence
buckets.blurb: Needs a prior subagent's output

items.text: Search three separate topic areas
items.why: Independent inputs, independent outputs.

items.text: Summarise the findings that those three searches produced
items.why: Its input does not exist yet.

items.text: Analyse each of four uploaded documents
items.why: Per-document work shares nothing.

items.text: Cross-check claim A against claim B, where B comes from another subagent
items.why: A genuine data dependency.

items.text: Draft one section per chapter of an agreed outline
items.why: The outline is the shared input; the drafts are not inputs to each other.

items.text: Produce the outline those drafters will use
items.why: It is the shared input, so it has to exist first.

items.text: Verify the citations in a completed draft
items.why: Needs the draft.

items.text: Run the same extraction over five regional datasets
items.why: Sectioning the same work across inputs, not chaining.

steps.hint: For each item, ask what its input is and whether that input is already sitting in the coordinator's hand.
steps.explanation: The test is purely whether the item's input is another item's output. Independence is what makes work parallelisable — not similarity, and not how long it takes. Note the pairs: the searches are parallel and their summary is not; the drafts are parallel and the outline they share is not.
steps.tip: Parallel spawning means several delegations in one coordinator response. Two delegations in two consecutive turns is sequential no matter what you call it.

steps.title: Drill: passing data across the isolation boundary
Drill: why everything transits the hub
Drill: scheduling dependent and independent work

title: Decomposition & Enforcement Points
outcome: Choose the layer a rule has to live in for the guarantee it needs, and decompose work into passes whose ordering you can defend.
objectives: Explain why a rule in a prompt and the same rule in code are different guarantees
Place a stated rule at the right enforcement point among prompt, examples, hooks, schema and code
Tell a PreToolUse concern apart from a PostToolUse one
Order a pipeline correctly and recognise which stages are genuinely commutative
Decide between a fixed chain and adaptive decomposition

steps.title: Where a rule lives determines whether it holds
steps.body:
A rule written into a prompt is followed most of the time. The same rule expressed in code, in a hook, or in a schema is followed every time. That is a categorical difference, not a difference of degree, and no amount of rewording closes it.

So the architect's question is never 'how do I word this'. It is 'what is the consequence of the one time it does not hold'. If the answer involves money moving, access being granted, an irreversible action, or an ordering that downstream code depends on, the rule has to sit somewhere the model cannot route around it.

The corollary is just as important: rules whose occasional violation costs nothing — tone, house spelling, formatting preferences — belong in the prompt. Enforcing those in code is its own kind of over-engineering.

callout.body: Prompt: the model is asked. Schema, hook, or code: the model is not consulted. Pick by consequence of failure, not by how important the rule feels.

steps.tip: Beware answers that promise a guarantee from better prompt wording. 'Add a strongly worded instruction' is almost never the right fix for a rule that must hold every time.

steps.title: Before or after the tool runs?
steps.prompt: Each of these needs a hook. Decide whether it inspects a pending tool call or transforms a result that already came back.

buckets.label: PreToolUse
buckets.blurb: Inspects a pending call; can block or redirect it

buckets.label: PostToolUse
buckets.blurb: Transforms a tool result before the model sees it

items.text: Block a call whose amount exceeds a threshold
items.why: It must happen before execution, or the money already moved.

items.text: Convert epoch timestamps to a single date format
items.why: The result is the thing that needs fixing.

items.text: Redirect a policy-violating call to escalation instead
items.why: Same turn, different destination — only possible before the call runs.

items.text: Strip unused fields from a verbose response
items.why: Context hygiene on the way in.

items.text: Require confirmation before an irreversible action
items.why: The gate is the entire point.

items.text: Redact secrets that a tool returned
items.why: Keep them out of the context window entirely.

items.text: Reject a call whose arguments fail a business rule
items.why: Validation of a call is a pre-execution concern.

items.text: Attach the source URL to each returned record
items.why: Provenance enrichment applied to the result.

steps.hint: Ask what the hook is protecting: the world, or the context window.
steps.explanation: PreToolUse protects the world — it is the only place you can stop something from happening. PostToolUse protects the context window and the model's reasoning — it shapes what the model gets to see. If undoing the action would be impossible or expensive, you needed pre.
steps.tip: If a PreToolUse hook blocks the call, there is no result for a PostToolUse hook to act on. Chaining a post hook as a safety net behind a pre hook does not give you two chances.

steps.title: Pick the enforcement point
steps.prompt: For each requirement, choose the layer that actually delivers the guarantee it needs — no stronger, no weaker.

buckets.label: System prompt
buckets.blurb: An instruction in the prompt

buckets.label: Few-shot examples
buckets.blurb: Worked examples in the prompt

buckets.label: PreToolUse hook
buckets.blurb: Inspects a pending tool call; can block or redirect

buckets.label: PostToolUse hook
buckets.blurb: Transforms a tool result before the model sees it

buckets.label: Schema
buckets.blurb: The tool's input schema or structured-output contract

buckets.label: Application code
buckets.blurb: Deterministic pipeline logic, outside any tool call

items.text: A refund above the policy cap must never execute
items.why: A specific pending call has to be stopped. Wording alone leaves a residual failure rate, and the failures involve money.

items.text: Three MCP servers return dates in three different formats
items.why: Normalise on the way in so the model never reasons over mixed formats.

items.text: A delete tool must never target production
items.why: Irreversible. The call itself is the thing to block.

items.text: Tool results arrive with 40 fields when 5 are used
items.why: Trim it before it reaches the window.

items.text: Extracted records must always carry a category from a closed set
items.why: An enum makes the invalid value unrepresentable rather than merely discouraged.

items.text: A field should be omitted rather than guessed when the source does not contain it
items.why: Make it nullable or optional. A prompt asking the model not to guess is fighting a required field.

items.text: The enrichment stage must not start until validation passes
items.why: Sequencing between pipeline stages. There is no tool call to intercept.

items.text: A batch must not be submitted until every input carries an id
items.why: A precondition on the caller's own flow, checked before anything is sent.

items.text: Prefer British spelling in user-facing copy
items.why: Cosmetic. An occasional miss costs nothing, and enforcing it in code is over-engineering.

items.text: Replies should stay warm when a customer is frustrated
items.why: Tone. Not mechanically enforceable, and not worth enforcing.

items.text: Classifications keep drifting between "bug" and "defect" for similar tickets
items.why: Inconsistent output on a judgement call is the textbook signal for examples.

items.text: Edge-case tickets get categorised inconsistently and no wording has fixed it
items.why: The pattern is easier to show than to state — and prose has already been tried.

items.text: The agent should stop after five failed attempts
items.why: A runaway guard belongs to the driver. It is not loop control and it is not a request to the model.

steps.hint: Work in two passes. First ask what kind of failure this is — a wrong action, a wrong shape, or a wrong style. Then ask what it costs when it happens once.
steps.explanation:
Three rules resolve almost every item. Match the fix to the failure: inconsistent judgement wants examples, a malformed value wants a schema, a forbidden action wants a gate. Fix at the earliest layer that can hold: a schema that makes a bad value unrepresentable beats a hook that catches it. And do not over-enforce: tone and spelling belong in the prompt, and putting them in code buys nothing.

Watch the pre-versus-code boundary. PreToolUse is for rules about a tool call that is about to happen. Application code is for rules about pipeline flow where no tool call is involved at all.
steps.tip: This step is the exam's second principle made mechanical. If an answer offers a guarantee and the mechanism is prompt wording, it is almost certainly the distractor.

steps.title: Order the extraction pipeline
steps.prompt: A document-processing pipeline has six stages. Put them in a workable order.

items.label: Accept the document
Extract document-level metadata
Extract the field data
Check the extraction
Add external data
Produce the final record

why.ingest: Nothing can run before the document exists in the pipeline.
why.metadata: Document-level metadata and field extraction are independent reads of the same source. Convention puts metadata first — some designs force it with a required first call — but it is a convention, not a dependency, so the two orders are both accepted.
why.extract: The field data is what everything downstream operates on.
why.validate: Load-bearing: validation has to precede enrichment. Enriching a bad extraction spends an external call and then buries the error underneath the enriched data.
why.enrich: External data is added only to an extraction already known to be sound.
why.emit: Last by definition — it is the pipeline's output.

steps.hint: Two of these stages are genuinely interchangeable. The other four have a hard constraint each, and one of those constraints is the reason a broken record ever reaches production.
steps.explanation: The two constraints that matter are validate-before-enrich and emit-last. Enriching first is the expensive mistake: you pay for the external lookup and you make the original defect harder to see. Metadata and field extraction are commutative, which is why both orders grade as correct here rather than one being declared canonical.
steps.tip: When a step's inputs do not include another step's outputs, the ordering between them is yours to choose — and that is also the signal that they could run in parallel.

steps.title: Order the review passes
steps.prompt: A pull request touches several files and needs a thorough review. Order the passes.

items.label: Analyse auth.ts in isolation
Analyse database.ts in isolation
Analyse routes.ts in isolation
Analyse the relationships across the files

why.f-auth: One file at a time keeps analysis depth even. A single sweeping pass goes deep on the first files and shallow on the rest, and will flag a pattern in one file that it waved through in another.
why.f-db: Nothing about auth.ts informs this pass, and nothing here informs the routes pass. The per-file passes are genuinely commutative.
why.f-routes: Same. Because these three share no inputs or outputs, any order of them is correct — which is also exactly why they can be parallel subagents.
why.integration: Last, and the only real constraint here. Cross-file defects — inconsistent types, cycles, boundary mismatches — are invisible from inside any single file, and this pass consumes the per-file findings.

steps.hint: Only one of these four has an input that another produces.
steps.explanation: Any order of the three per-file passes is accepted, because they are independent by construction. The integration pass must come last: its input is the output of the others. Recognising commutativity is not pedantry — it is the same test that tells you which delegations can be spawned in a single turn.
steps.tip: An ordering question where several orders are equally correct is telling you something about the architecture, not testing your memory of a canonical sequence.

steps.title: Fixed chain or adaptive decomposition?
steps.prompt: For each task, decide whether the subtasks can be enumerated before you start or have to be generated from what you find along the way.

buckets.label: Fixed chain
buckets.blurb: Steps enumerable up front

buckets.label: Adaptive
buckets.blurb: Subtasks generated from intermediate results

items.text: Monthly compliance review against a fixed template
items.why: The same steps every month.

items.text: Add tests to this legacy service
items.why: You have to map the code before you know what the work is.

items.text: Migrate 60 files to a new import style
items.why: Mechanical and enumerable. Volume is not complexity.

items.text: Trace an intermittent production failure
items.why: Each finding redirects the next step.

items.text: Extract the same twelve fields from each invoice
items.why: The fields are known before the first document arrives.

items.text: Assess whether a third-party library is safe to adopt
items.why: The scope emerges: licence, maintenance, transitive dependencies, known vulnerabilities — and which of those matter depends on what you find.

items.text: Weekly summarisation of a fixed set of dashboards
items.why: Fixed inputs, fixed output shape.

items.text: Work out why two reports disagree
items.why: The cause of the discrepancy determines where to look next.

steps.hint: Could you write the list of subtasks down before you begin, and would it still be right at the end?
steps.explanation: The test is whether intermediate results change what the remaining work is. If they do not, a fixed chain is cheaper, more debuggable and more repeatable. Note that none of these are decided by size — a 60-file migration is fixed, and a single ambiguous bug report is adaptive.
steps.tip: Adaptive decomposition is the more powerful tool and the more common wrong answer. Reach for it only when the work genuinely cannot be enumerated.

steps.title: Drill: what happens when a gate fires

title: Tool Descriptions & Selection
outcome: Fix misrouting at the description rather than in front of it, choose the tool_choice mode a requirement actually needs, and reach for the right built-in tool.
objectives: Explain why the description is the model's routing table
Decide what belongs in a tool description and what only costs tokens
Map a stated guarantee to the correct tool_choice mode
Choose among the built-in file and search tools
Sequence an incremental investigation instead of bulk-reading a module

steps.title: The description is the routing table
steps.body:
The model chooses a tool by reading its description. That is the whole mechanism. There is no registry it consults, no type system that disambiguates, and no memory of which tool worked last time.

So when two tools have descriptions that overlap, the model misroutes — and the symptom looks exactly like a model failure. It is an authoring failure. The cheap fix is nearly always in the text: differentiate the descriptions, rename the tools so their names do not read as synonyms, and add an explicit clause saying when to prefer one over the other.

Read each description as if it were the only thing you knew about the tool, because for the model, it is.

callout.body: Putting a routing classifier in front of a confused toolset, before rewriting the descriptions, is a documented instant-elimination. You have added a component, a failure mode and a latency hop to work around text you could have edited.

steps.tip: When a question describes tool confusion, scan the options for the one that edits the descriptions. It is usually correct, and it is usually the least impressive-sounding option on the list.

steps.title: What belongs in a tool description?
steps.prompt: You are writing the description for an MCP tool. Sort each candidate line by whether it helps the model choose and call the tool correctly.

buckets.label: Include
buckets.blurb: The model acts on it

buckets.label: Omit
buckets.blurb: Costs tokens in every request and changes nothing

items.text: What the tool returns, field by field
items.why: Half of the selection decision is knowing what comes back.

items.text: Accepted input formats, with a concrete example value
items.why: Removes the guess. An example is worth several sentences of specification.

items.text: When to prefer this tool over the similar one
items.why: The disambiguation clause — the single highest-value line when two tools overlap.

items.text: Known limits and edge cases, such as the maximum range or what an empty result means
items.why: Prevents misuse, and prevents an empty result being read as a failure.

items.text: What the tool does not do
items.why: A boundary is as informative as a capability.

items.text: The language the server is written in
items.why: The model never acts on it.

items.text: Which internal service it proxies
items.why: Implementation detail, paid for in every single request.

items.text: The maintainer and the changelog
items.why: Metadata for humans, sitting in an artifact humans do not read.

items.text: The server's retry and timeout policy
items.why: The model does not choose it. Surface failures through structured errors instead.

items.text: A paragraph about how important accuracy is on this tool
items.why: Exhortation in a routing artifact. It displaces the clauses that would actually have helped.

steps.hint: For each line, ask whether a different value for it would change which tool the model picks or what arguments it sends.
steps.explanation: A description earns its tokens by changing a decision: which tool, what arguments, how to read the result. Provenance, implementation and encouragement change none of those, and they are not free — the whole toolset's descriptions ship on every request.
steps.tip: Descriptions are context. A verbose toolset is a context-window problem before it is a documentation problem.

steps.title: Which tool_choice mode?
steps.prompt: Each turn below has a stated requirement. Pick the tool_choice mode that delivers it.

buckets.label: auto
buckets.blurb: The model decides whether to call a tool

buckets.label: any
buckets.blurb: A tool call is guaranteed; the model picks which

buckets.label: a named tool
buckets.blurb: This exact tool is called

buckets.label: none
buckets.blurb: No tool is called this turn

items.text: A support agent that sometimes answers from knowledge and sometimes looks things up
items.why: The mixed case is the default case.

items.text: An extraction job that must never return prose
items.why: Guarantees a tool call without dictating which one.

items.text: A pipeline where metadata extraction must be the first action
items.why: Forces the first step deterministically rather than requesting it.

items.text: Several extraction tools, and any structured result is acceptable
items.why: The guarantee is on the shape of the output, not on which tool produced it.

items.text: A summarisation turn where tool access would only be a distraction
items.why: Suppress the surface for the turn instead of hoping the model declines.

items.text: A drafting step that must produce text, with tools still configured for later turns
items.why: Same, and it saves tearing down and rebuilding the tool configuration per turn.

items.text: Every response must call validate_record before anything else
items.why: A named requirement needs the named mode.

items.text: A general assistant with a small, well-described toolset
items.why: Nothing here needs a guarantee, and forcing calls would make the assistant worse.

steps.hint: Two questions in order: is a tool call required this turn, and does it matter which tool?
steps.explanation:
The four modes answer two independent questions. Is a call required? If no and tools are irrelevant this turn, none; if no but tools may help, auto. If yes, does the specific tool matter? If not, any; if so, name it.

The common error is reaching for a named tool when only the output shape was required — that removes the model's ability to route correctly and buys a guarantee you did not need.
steps.tip: tool_choice is an enforcement mechanism, not a hint. If a requirement says 'must' and the proposed answer is prompt wording, the mode was the real answer.

steps.title: Which built-in tool?
steps.prompt: Pick the tool that does each job most directly.

buckets.label: Glob
buckets.blurb: Find files by name or path pattern

buckets.label: Grep
buckets.blurb: Find files by their contents

buckets.label: Read
buckets.blurb: Load a file's contents

buckets.label: Write
buckets.blurb: Create or wholesale replace a file

buckets.label: Edit
buckets.blurb: Replace a portion of an existing file

buckets.label: Bash
buckets.blurb: Run a command

items.text: Find every file matching **/*.test.tsx
items.why: A name and path pattern.

items.text: Find where parseInvoice is defined
items.why: Content search.

items.text: Find every import of a deprecated module
items.why: Content search across files.

items.text: List the components directory by extension
items.why: Pattern over paths, not over contents.

items.text: Load a config file in full in order to reason about it
items.why: You want the bytes, and you already know the path.

items.text: Create a new migration file that does not exist yet
items.why: Nothing to edit.

items.text: Change one constant inside an existing 900-line file
items.why: A targeted replacement. Rewriting the file risks losing everything you did not re-emit.

items.text: Run the test suite
items.why: An actual command.

items.text: Stage and commit changes
items.why: An actual command.

items.text: Replace a file's contents wholesale after computing the new version
items.why: The fallback path when a targeted edit is not applicable.

items.text: Check whether a directory contains any YAML at all
items.why: Existence by pattern.

items.text: Find the file that produced a given error string
items.why: Search by the message text.

steps.hint: Glob asks which files. Grep asks which files contain. Everything else is about what you do once you know.
steps.explanation: The recurring wrong answer is Bash. Shelling out to run find or grep works, but it discards the dedicated tool's structured output, its permissions story and its result limits — and it is the answer a question is usually testing you to reject. Between Write and Edit the rule is scope: replace part of a file with Edit, replace the whole thing with Write.
steps.tip: If an option runs a shell command to do something a first-class tool already does, treat it as a distractor until proven otherwise.

steps.title: Sequence an incremental investigation
steps.prompt: You need to understand an unfamiliar module before changing it. Order the steps.

items.label: Search for the definition or export you are starting from
Read the files that search found
Search for imports and call sites of what you just read
Read the consumers
Decide whether the picture is complete, and repeat if not

why.grep-entry: Start narrow. The first search is the only one whose terms you supply yourself.
why.read-entry: Read only what the search returned — not the directory it lives in.
why.grep-usage: The search terms for this step come out of the previous step's file. That dependency is what makes the pattern incremental rather than a fixed recipe.
why.read-consumers: Now you are reading files chosen by evidence rather than by proximity.
why.assess: Loop control. Either the picture is complete or you have new terms for another pass.

steps.hint: Every search term after the first one comes from somewhere. Where?
steps.explanation: Each step's inputs are the previous step's outputs, so there is no valid alternate ordering. The tempting wrong approach is to read every file in the module first and search afterwards — which fills the context window with files that turn out to be irrelevant, and leaves the genuinely important ones buried in the middle of it. That is a context-economics failure wearing a tool-selection hat.
steps.tip: If the sweep is going to be large, this is exactly the work to delegate: spend a subagent's window on the search and have it return the map rather than the files.

steps.title: When an MCP tool loses to a built-in
steps.body:
A predictable failure: you ship an MCP tool, and the model keeps using a built-in that does something superficially similar instead. Nothing is broken. The built-in's description sounds like it covers the job, and the MCP tool's does not say otherwise.

The fix is in the MCP tool's description, and it is specific: say what this tool reaches that the built-in cannot. The live system rather than the checkout. The authoritative record rather than a local copy. Data that is not on disk at all.

This is the same lesson as overlapping tool descriptions, with a harder edge — you are competing against a description you do not control, so yours has to carry the distinction on its own.

callout.body: Wrong instinct: remove the built-in so the model has no choice. Right instinct: state the boundary in the description. Stripping tools to force a route is the same over-correction as adding a classifier to fix one.

steps.tip: Whenever an answer proposes removing capability to steer behaviour, check whether describing the boundary would have worked. Usually it would.

steps.title: Drill: why the model confuses two tools
Drill: fixing selection at the description

title: MCP Surface: Primitives, Errors & Scope
outcome: Choose between a tool, a resource and a prompt, categorise a failure correctly enough to pick the right response, and put server configuration in the right file.
objectives: Decide whether a capability should be a tool, a resource or a prompt
Sort failures into transient, validation, business, permission — and the empty result that is not a failure at all
Say which failures a retry can help and which it cannot
Sequence failure handling between a subagent and its coordinator
Place MCP server configuration in project scope, personal scope, or nowhere

steps.title: Tool, resource, or prompt?
steps.prompt: You are designing an MCP server. Decide how each capability should be exposed.

buckets.label: Tool
buckets.blurb: The agent acts

buckets.label: Resource
buckets.blurb: The agent reads it for context

buckets.label: Prompt
buckets.blurb: A reusable template that gets invoked

items.text: Create an issue in the tracker
items.why: A state-changing action.

items.text: The catalogue of every open issue, for navigation
items.why: Read-only orientation. It exists to remove exploratory calls.

items.text: The database schema
items.why: Structure the agent reads in order to plan its queries.

items.text: Run a migration
items.why: An action with consequences.

items.text: The team's code-review checklist, as a fill-in template
items.why: A reusable prompt skeleton. Not data, and not an action.

items.text: Fetch one customer record by id
items.why: A parameterised lookup the model drives by supplying the id.

items.text: The internal API reference documentation
items.why: Reference material the agent reads, not a call it makes.

items.text: Post a message to a channel
items.why: An action.

items.text: A standard incident-report template the agent fills in
items.why: A named, reusable template, invoked rather than read.

items.text: A hierarchical map of the project's document tree
items.why: The map case exactly — one read replaces a walk.

steps.hint: The hard pair is 'fetch one record' against 'the catalogue of records'. What is different about who supplies the parameters?
steps.explanation: Actionable means tool; informational means resource. Fetching a record by id is a tool because the model supplies the parameter and drives the call. The catalogue is a resource because it is context the agent reads to know what exists in the first place. Prompts are the third thing: neither data nor action, but a reusable template that a human or an agent invokes by name.
steps.tip: If the agent has to find out what exists before it can act, that is a resource — and shipping one is usually cheaper than the exploratory tool calls it replaces.

steps.title: Categorise the failure
steps.prompt: Each of these came back from a tool call. Sort them by what kind of outcome they actually are.

buckets.label: Transient
buckets.blurb: Nothing is wrong with the request

buckets.label: Validation
buckets.blurb: The input was malformed

buckets.label: Business rule
buckets.blurb: The system worked and said no

buckets.label: Permission
buckets.blurb: The caller is not allowed

buckets.label: Not an error
buckets.blurb: A successful call with nothing in it

items.text: The orders API timed out
items.why: Retry with backoff. Nothing about the request needs to change.

items.text: A 503 from an upstream service
items.why: The service is unavailable right now, not wrong.

items.text: The connection was reset mid-request
items.why: Infrastructure, not semantics.

items.text: A required field was missing from the call
items.why: Retrying unchanged repeats the failure exactly. Fix the input first.

items.text: A date arrived in an unparseable format
items.why: The call is malformed and will stay malformed.

items.text: An enum value outside the accepted set was supplied
items.why: Same shape — and a schema could have made it unrepresentable.

items.text: The refund exceeds the policy cap
items.why: The system worked correctly and refused. Explain and offer an alternative; do not retry.

items.text: The account is outside the eligible window for this operation
items.why: A rule fired. Retrying is asking the same question again.

items.text: The caller's credentials do not grant access to this record
items.why: Not fixable by the agent. This is escalation material.

items.text: The API key lacks the required scope
items.why: A human has to change something outside the agent's reach.

items.text: The search ran and matched zero records
items.why: A successful call with an empty result. Miscategorising this drives retry loops and false reports that the search is unavailable.

items.text: The customer has no orders in the last 90 days
items.why: The same shape in business-flavoured wording — which is exactly why this is the one candidates get wrong.

steps.hint: One bucket is not a failure mode at all. Two items belong in it, and the second is dressed up to look like it belongs somewhere else.
steps.explanation:
The category determines the response, which is why getting it wrong is expensive: transient wants a bounded retry, validation wants the input corrected first, business wants an explanation and an alternative, permission wants a human.

The fifth bucket is the one that earns its place. An empty result is a successful call. Treating it as an error produces retry loops against a query that will keep returning nothing, and tells the user the system is broken when it is working.
steps.tip: "Treats an empty result as an error" is an instant-elimination rule. If an option's error handling cannot distinguish 'found nothing' from 'could not look', it is wrong.

steps.title: Will a retry help?
steps.prompt: Same outcomes, one question: does resending the identical call have any chance of a different result?

buckets.label: Retry as-is
buckets.blurb: Backoff and send the same call again

buckets.label: Do not retry as-is
buckets.blurb: Something else has to change first, or nothing needs to happen

items.text: The orders API timed out
items.why: The only class where the identical call can succeed on the next attempt.

items.text: A 503 from an upstream service
items.why: Bounded backoff. Unbounded retry turns a blip into an outage of your own making.

items.text: The connection was reset mid-request
items.why: Same call, different moment.

items.text: A required field was missing from the call
items.why: The input has to be fixed. Retrying unchanged is a loop with a fixed outcome.

items.text: An enum value outside the accepted set was supplied
items.why: Correct the value, then call again. That is a new call, not a retry.

items.text: The refund exceeds the policy cap
items.why: The rule will fire again. A different action is needed, not another attempt.

items.text: The caller's credentials do not grant access to this record
items.why: Nothing the agent can do changes the answer.

items.text: The search ran and matched zero records
items.why: There is nothing to retry. The call succeeded.

items.text: The customer has no orders in the last 90 days
items.why: Retrying a successful empty query is the purest form of wasted latency.

steps.hint: Only one of the five categories from the previous step lands in the retry bucket.
steps.explanation:
Retry is for transient failures and nothing else. Validation needs a corrected input, business needs a different action, permission needs a person, and an empty result needs nothing.

Watch how the two empty-result rows behave here. Suppressing them silently is the first bug; someone notices, adds a retry, and turns it into a second one. Neither is handling — the fix is to report the empty result as a successful outcome with no matches.
steps.tip: A retry that is not bounded is not a recovery strategy. 'Retry with exponential backoff' should always come with a ceiling in the answer you pick.

steps.title: Propagate a subagent failure
steps.prompt: A subagent's tool call fails partway through a multi-agent run. Order the handling.

items.label: The subagent retries locally, once or twice
The subagent packages a structured error alongside whatever partial results it has
The error is returned to the coordinator
The coordinator decides: retry modified, accept partial, or reroute
The gap is marked in the final synthesis

why.local-retry: Bounded local recovery comes first because propagating a transient blip costs a coordinator round-trip for nothing. Bounded is the operative word — one or two attempts, then tell the hub.
why.structure: Partial results are worth more than a failure flag. Package what did succeed with a categorised error rather than discarding the work.
why.propagate: The coordinator is the only component that can see the whole run, so it is the only one that can decide what a failure means for the goal.
why.decide: Retry with modified inputs, accept the partial result, or route the work to a different subagent. This is the hub earning its place in the architecture.
why.annotate: Last, because until the coordinator has decided, you do not yet know what the gap in the output is. Skipping this step is silent degradation — a confident-looking answer with a hole in it.

steps.hint: Two of these are the classic failure modes, and they sit at opposite ends. One of them is doing too much before telling anyone; the other is not doing something at the end.
steps.explanation:
The two ways this goes wrong are symmetric. Propagate first, and every hiccup escalates to the coordinator — the retry logic may as well not exist. Retry without a bound, and a failing subagent hangs the run while the hub sees nothing. 'One or two, then tell the hub' is the shape.

Dropping the final annotation is the quieter failure: the synthesis reads as complete, and nothing in it says which part is missing.
steps.tip: Any design where a partial failure disappears before the output is produced is wrong, however well the earlier steps are handled.

steps.title: Where does this MCP configuration go?
steps.prompt: Sort each item into the configuration file it belongs in.

buckets.label: Project scope
buckets.blurb: Committed with the repository

buckets.label: Personal scope
buckets.blurb: Your user-level config, not committed

buckets.label: Neither
buckets.blurb: Does not belong in a config file at all

items.text: The issue-tracker server every engineer on the team needs
items.why: A shared standard. Committing it means it arrives with the checkout.

items.text: A server you are prototyping this afternoon
items.why: Not the team's problem yet.

items.text: A reference to a token via an environment variable
items.why: The reference is committable precisely because the value is not.

items.text: The token's actual value
items.why: Never in either file. Personal scope is still a file on disk, and it is still not a secret store.

items.text: A server that only works against your local database
items.why: Machine-specific. Committing it breaks everyone else's setup.

items.text: A vendor server the whole team was told to adopt
items.why: Shared requirement, shared file.

items.text: A personal override of a shared server's settings
items.why: Overrides stay local. Editing the shared file changes it for everyone, which is not what an override means.

items.text: Your API key pasted into the env block just for now
items.why: The same trap phrased the way it actually happens.

steps.hint: Two of these are the same mistake written twice. Personal scope is private in the sense of 'not shared with the team' — which is not the same sense a credential needs.
steps.explanation:
The axis is who needs it: everyone, or you. Shared requirements get committed so a fresh checkout works; machine-specific and experimental things stay local; personal overrides stay local by definition.

The third bucket exists for the trap. 'Not committed' is not the same as 'secret'. A token belongs in the environment, referenced from the config — which is why the reference itself is perfectly safe to commit.
steps.tip: When a config question offers 'put it in the personal file so it does not get committed' as a way to handle a credential, that is the distractor.
